chapter.title: Overview
chapter.simpara: There are several ways to install, operate and use QALIPSIS, depending on your use case, load needs and IT-landscape complexity.
This section discusses different approaches, including the benefits and consequences of each approach.

If you find yourself in a situation that requires professional support to assist in the preparation, installation and operation phases, do not hesitate to contact our team at https://qalipsis.io/#contact.

chapter.title: Ephemeral installations
chapter.simpara: Ephemeral installations of QALIPSIS are designed to be directly embedded into third-party tools or for use in continuous integration/continuous deployment (CI/CD) applications.
However, the ease of build and lightness of an ephermeral installation makes it a very portable alternative for verifying test systems and performing demos.
The QALIPSIS Gradle Plugin provides the task package-ephemeral to create an archive of your project.
To configure an ephemeral installation for project deployment:

listitem.simpara: In your QALIPSIS project, execute the task package-ephemeral which will assemble your project and create a ZIP file build/install/your-project-name-your-version-qalipsis.jar.
The .jar file is a self-executable archive that contains your scenario, classes, and all required dependencies to execute your QALIPSIS project.
Check that Java Runtime Environment 11+ is installed on your system as it is required to execute the archive.
Move the archived file to your folder of choice; navigate to that folder.
If operating on a Windows system, run java -jar your-project-name-your-version-qalipsis.jar --autostart.
If operating on a Linux, Unix or Mac system:
Run chmod +x your-project-name-your-version-qalipsis.jar
Run java -jar your-project-name-your-version-qalipsis.jar --autostart
QALIPSIS starts, executes the scenario(s) in the project, and quits.
The results of the project are displayed on the console but are not saved to QALIPSIS.
To save the results, export them to a file(s) using any of our exporters such as JUnit.

chapter.simpara: If you want to tweak the configuration, create and include in your project a qalipsis.yml or config/qalipsis.yml file that contains the part of the configuration that you want to overwrite.
Note that the config/qalipsis.yml file has precedence over the qalipsis.yml if both are present in the configuration.
If any of your use cases includes data that you want to retain in QALIPSIS for comparison, regression testing, or other use, you should use QALIPSIS in a non-persistent standalone installation.

section.title: Overview
section.simpara: Transient installations of QALIPSIS are designed for operations that do not need to keep a history of data. The most often used cases for transient installations include:

listitem.simpara: Continuous integration/continuous deployment (CI/CD) workflows where only the success or failure of the execution determines if the data passes through the quality gates.
End-to-end testing that does not require a history of data.

section.simpara: A transient installation is not a long-live process.

listitem.simpara: QALIPSIS starts a campaign execution on demand and stops immediately after the campaign’s completion.
To keep external logs of the campaign execution, export the results as a file or to a third-party platform.

section.simpara: If any of your use cases includes data that you want to retain in QALIPSIS for comparison, regression testing, or other use, you should use QALIPSIS in a non-persistent standalone installation.

section.title: Configuring a transient instalation for project deployment
section.simpara: The QALIPSIS Gradle Plugin provides tasks to create an installable archive from your project. It proposes a unique task to create a ZIP archive and a TAR.GZ archive. The installation is similar whether using a ZIP archive or a TAR.GZ archive; installation using a ZIP archive are described in this section.
In your QALIPSIS project, execute the task package-standalone which will assemble your project and create a ZIP file: build/install/qalipsis-your-project-name.zip.
The ZIP archive contains your scenario, classes, and all required dependencies to execute your QALIPSIS project.

listitem.simpara: Add the ZIP archive to your installation folder of choice and uncompress it: unzip qalipsis-your-project-name.zip or use another compression tool of your choice.
Navigate to the QALIPSIS folder on your system:
Linux, Unix or Mac systems:`/your/installation/folder/qalipsis-your-project-name`
Windows systems:`C:\your\installation\folder\qalipsis-your-project-name on Windows`
Start QALIPSIS:
On Linux, Unix or Mac systems:
Run chmod +x -R
Run ./qalipsis.sh --autostart
On Windows systems:
Run qalipsis.bat --autostart

section.simpara: An additional argument is not required, as the transient, non-persistent execution, is the default.

listitem.simpara: QALIPSIS starts, executes the scenario(s) in the project, and quits.
The results of the project are displayed on the console but are not saved to QALIPSIS.
To save the results, export them results to a file(s) using an exporter such as JUnit.

section.simpara: If you want to tweak the configuration, create and include in your project a qalipsis.yml or config/qalipsis.yml file that contains the part of the configuration that you want to overwrite.
Note that the config/qalipsis.yml file has precedence over the qalipsis.yml if both are present in the configuration.
If any of your use cases includes data that you want to retain in QALIPSIS for comparison, regression testing, or other use, you should use QALIPSIS in a non-persistent standalone installation.

section.title: Overview
section.simpara: Standalone installations of QALIPSIS are designed for low to normal load operations that require data tracking. They allow you to tweak the deployment configuration to track the history of executions.
A standalone installation: * Executes all QALIPSIS components as in a transient installation. * Provides durability in data and history tracking. * Provides REST endpoints to interact with QALIPSIS.

QALIPSIS stores the master data and the time-series data generated by the scenario (events, meters) in separate databases. The master data requires transactional consistency and the time-series data requires high throughput and strong calculation capabilities. Separate databases allow for the different requirements.
For sake of simplicity, this guide will use PostgreSQL for both groups of data.

If you want to configure a different database for the time-series data, refer to the applicable plugin section for details (elasticsearch, influxDB, timescaleDB, and graphite are currently supported).
This section will guide you through the configuration of a installation to a persistent standalone installation.
While this installation requires the installation of a PostgreSQL database, we will not go into those details which are provided in the official PostgreSQL documentation.

section.title: Prepare the installation
section.simpara: Execute the following steps as for a installation:

listitem.simpara: In your QALIPSIS project, execute the task package-standalone to create a ZIP file: build/install/qalipsis-your-project-name.zip.
Add the ZIP archive to your installation folder of choice and uncompress it: unzip qalipsis-your-project-name.zip.
Install your PostgreSQL database
Create a user with the name qalipsis.
Create a database with the name qalipsis.
Connect to the database qalipsis with your admin user (generally postgres).
Execute the following:

section.title: Configure the database for the master data

listitem.simpara: Navigate to your installation folder where the qalipsis.sh and qalipsis.bat files are located.
Ceate a qalipsis.yml file to configure access to the database for the master data:

section.screen: datasources: default: url: jdbc:postgresql://your-database-host:5432/qalipsis username: qalipsis password: the_password_of_your_qalipsis_user maximumPoolSize: 4 minimumIdle: 2

callout.para: Replace the host, port, username, and password values with the appropriate values for you configuration.

The maximumPoolSize and minimumIdle are the REST endpoints and depend on your number of users. Increase the value of maximumPoolSize if you observe errors or a high latency when using the REST endpoints.

section.title: Configure the database for the events and meters
section.simpara: The connection to PostgreSQL for events and meters is provided by the timescale plugin, which is embedded by default in all the head, transient and standalone installation you will build with the QALIPSIS Gradle plugin.
In the file qalipsis.yml add the following details:
section.screen:
events: export.timescaledb: enabled: true host: your-database-host port: 5432 database: qalipsis username: qalipsis password: the_password_of_your_qalipsis_user schema: meters
provider.timescaledb: enabled: true host: your-database-host port: 5432 database: qalipsis username: qalipsis password: the_password_of_your_qalipsis_user schema: meters
meters: export.timescaledb: enabled: true host: your-database-host port: 5432 database: qalipsis username: qalipsis password: the_password_of_your_qalipsis_user schema: meters
provider.timescaledb: enabled: true host: your-database-host port: 5432 database: qalipsis username: qalipsis password: the_password_of_your_qalipsis_user schema: meters

section.title: Start QALIPSIS
section.simpara: Navigate to the QALIPSIS folder on your system:

listitem.simpara: Linux, Unix or Mac systems:`/your/installation/folder/qalipsis-your-project-name`
Windows systems:`C:\your\installation\folder\qalipsis-your-project-name on Windows`

section.simpara: Start QALIPSIS:

listitem.simpara: On Linux, Unix or Mac systems:
Run chmod +x -R
Run ./qalipsis.sh --autostart
On Windows systems:
Run qalipsis.bat --autostart

section.simpara: QALIPSIS will start and provide REST endpoints on port 5432.
Open source QALIPSIS OSS does not support native security. For a native secured installation, we encourage you to contact the QALIPSIS team to request a license for QALIPSIS Enterprise.

section.title: Overview
section.simpara: Clusters provide a scalable and durable installation of QALIPSIS, allowing increased load needs, more scenarios and more users.
In a cluster, QALIPSIS is split into several components which collaborate using third-parties systems to exchange data.

Heads are the components users use to interact with QALIPSIS. Factories are hidden and inaccessible to users. Factories only initiate outbound connections which allows them to securely run behind firewalls without limitation.
Heads should remain active permanently to provide the best user experience. Factories can remain active or come and go on demand without limiting the campaign results analysis.
Clusters can also be deployed in Kubernetes or OpenShift or in a hybrid system with some factories on physical machines and others on virtual machines.
The following sections describe how to install QALIPSIS as clusters of different forms that provide different levels of scalability and availability.

section.title: Light cluster
section.simpara: A light cluster requires minimal effort for installation; however, it is limited in terms of load capabilitiy.
The main advantage of a light cluster installation is to provide a secure geographical/network-based distribution of load and data consumption for private networks.
A light cluster consists of:

listitem.simpara: One instance of a head.
Several instances of factories.
A PostgreSQL database.
A Redis instance (or a Redis cluster) to host shared data and exchange messages between the QALIPSIS head and factories.

section.title: High-load cluster
section.simpara: A high-load cluster requires significant effort for installation and operation; however, it is extremely scalable.

listitem.simpara: A high-load cluster is only possible for enterprise installations. If you are interested in deploying a high-load cluster, contact the QALIPSIS team to request a license for QALIPSIS Enterprise.

section.simpara: A high-load cluster consists of:

listitem.simpara: One instance of a head.
Several instances of factories.
A PostgreSQL database.
One or more clusters of databases for the time-series data.
A Redis cluster to host shared data.
A Kafka cluster to exchange messages between the QALIPSIS head and factories.

chapter.title: Amendable installations
chapter.simpara: The purpose of an amendable installation is to allow you to add scenarios to the installation without having to rebuild a new installation archive after each change.
As a prerequesite, you need to install QALIPSIS as described in the transient, standalone or cluster installation sections.

When packaging an installation, you do not need to create a scenario for the project. An "empty" installation is a valid installation. Executing QALIPSIS on an "empty" installation will return an exit code different from 0 and a no scenario found warning.
When your QALIPSIS installation is complete:

listitem.simpara: Create a new QALIPSIS project using the QALIPSIS Gradle plugin
Create a scenario in your project.
Add implementation or runtime dependencies to your Gradle project, as you would with any Gradle project.
Execute the task package-scenario on your Gradle module, which will assemble your project and create a ZIP file:
This archive contains your scenario and all the non-QALIPSIS dependencies you have manually added to your Gradle project.
Copy and paste the archive to a folder with an existing installation of QALIPSIS transient, standalone or factory of a cluster.
Uncompress the file: unzip qalipsis-scenario-your-project-name.zip or by using another compression tool of your choice.
Copy each file in the expanded folder into the folder with scenarios of your existing installation.
If it is still running, restart QALIPSIS to load the new scenarios.

chapter.simpara: Different scenarios might require different dependencies; pay attention to the conflict of versions when you overwrite existing files inan existing installation.
To avoid conflicts, use the same versions of dependencies across all scenarios/projects deployed in the same installation.

chapter.title: Containers
chapter.simpara: Docker images from QALIPSIS work like prepared installations that you can amend with your own scenarios and configuration.
Three images are available on the Docker hub free to all QALIPSIS users:

listitem.simpara: qalipsis-oss:<version|latest> to start a container as a transient or standalone application.
qalipsis-oss-head:<version|latest> contains only the components to execute a head in a cluster.
qalipsis-oss-factory:<version|latest> contains only the components to execute a factory in a cluster.

section.title: Prepare the images
section.simpara: While the images available on the Docker hub are ready to use, empty standalone and factory images cannot do much for you.
You can add your own scenarios and dependencies as prepared for an Amendable installation with the following Dockerfile:

section.title: Where can you use the images?
section.simpara: All of the images are tested to run as Docker, Kubernetes or OpenShift containers and can be configured with additional program arguments or a mounted configuration file.
You can refer to the official documentation for each platform to learn how to attach a configuration file and arguments to the execution of QALIPSIS.
